in: Films, Blender Foundation

Big Buck Bunny

Sign in to edit

Big Buck Bunny

Premiere date

April 10, 2008

Runtime

9 minutes

Directed by

Sacha Goedegebure

Music by

Jan Morgenstern

Produced by

Ton Roosendaal

Big Buck Bunny is a 2008 animated comedy short film featuring animals of the forest, made by the Blender Institute, part of the Blender Foundation.[1][2] Like the foundation's previous film, Elephants Dream, the film was made using Blender, a free and open-source software application for 3D computer modeling and animation developed by the same foundation. Unlike that earlier project, the tone and visuals departed from a cryptic story and dark visuals to one of comedy, cartoons, and light-heartedness.

It was released as an open-source film under the Creative Commons Attribution 2.5 license.[3]

Contents

  • 1 Plot
  • 2 Production
  • 3 Release
  • 4 Characters
  • 5 Legacy
  • 6 References
  • 7 External links

Plot[]

The plot follows a day in the life of Big Buck Bunny, during which time he meets three bullying rodents; the leader, Frankie, and his sidekicks Rinky and Gimera. The rodents amuse themselves by harassing helpless creatures of the forest by throwing fruits, nuts, and rocks at them.

After the rodents kill two butterflies with an apple and a rock, and then attack JC, he sets aside his gentle nature and orchestrates a complex plan to avenge the two butterflies.

Using a variety of traps, JC first dispatches Rinky and Gimera. Frankie, unaware of the fate of the other two, is seen taking off from a tree, and gliding towards a seemingly unsuspecting JC. Once airborne, Frankie triggers JC's final series of traps, causing Frankie to crash into a tree branch and plummet into a spike trap below. At the last moment, Frankie grabs onto what he believes is the branch of a small tree, but discovers it is just a twig JC is holding over the spikes. JC snatches up Frankie.

The film concludes with JC being pleased with himself as a butterfly flies past him holding a string, at the end of which is Frankie attached as a flying kite.

Production[]

Following Elephants Dream, Big Buck Bunny is the first project by the Blender Foundation to be created by the Blender Institute, a division of the foundation set up specifically to facilitate the creation of open content films and games.[4][5]

Work began in October 2007. The film was funded by the Blender Foundation, donations from the Blender community, pre-sales of the film's DVD and commercial sponsorship. Both the final product and production data, including animation data, characters and textures are released under the Creative Commons Attribution License. It was rendered on Sun Microsystems' grid computing facility Sun Grid.[6][7]

As in Elephants Dream, Blender developers worked extensively to improve the software in accordance with the needs of the movie team. Improvements were made in hair and fur rendering, the particle system, UV mapping, shading, the render pipeline, constraints, and skinning. Also introduced during the project was approximate ambient occlusion. These features were released to the public with Blender 2.46.[8]

Release[]

The film was officially released in an April 10, 2008 premiere in Amsterdam[9] while online movie downloads and files were released on May 30, 2008.[10]

The film's complete score was released on June 6, 2008 under the Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial-NoDerivativeWorks 3.0 license. It was available to download in MP3 and OGG Vorbis file formats.[11]

On May 2, 2012 the film was released on Nintendo Video in Europe as part of a special partnership with Nintendo and the Blender Foundation. It was split into three instalments, with one releasing each week. The film was recreated in stereoscopic 3D by Studio Lumikuu.[12]

High-resolution, stereoscopic, high-frame-rate versions of the film were released in 2013 by Janus Kristensen's Big and Ugly Rendering Project running on the Berkeley Open Infrastructure for Network Computing platform.[13]

The film was followed up with an open game titled Yo Frankie!, in August 2008.

Characters[]

The main character was also used in short films created by Studio Lumikuu and Renderfarm.fi: What is Renderfarm.fi? (2011) telling about the advantages of render farms and Big Buck Bunny loves CC (2012) promoting Creative Commons licences.[14]

JC's voice was performed by Jan Morgenstern, the composer of the film's soundtrack.

Frankie is also the player character in Yo Frankie!.

Legacy[]

Big Buck Bunny is commonly used to test video playback since it is royalty-free as well as being bright and colorful.[15]
